narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your high family income background and a solid LSAT score of 39.50, which stands out above the typical student's score of around 36.70. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 43%, tipping the decision toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance raised concerns about bar exam readiness. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also lower than the typical 5.7, added to that worry, though with less weight. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00—slightly under the average 3.21—still nudged the prediction toward success, as did your high family income and full-time study plans, which align with most students and helped counterbalance some risks. Compared to typical applicants, your LSAT is a standout strength that pushed strongly against failure, but your low decile rankings in school grades stood out as unusually weak, outweighing those positives in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—it's just the model highlighting patterns from past bar exam data where early and mid-school struggles often correlated with tougher outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring or academic coaching tailored to your full-time path. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews that weigh your LSAT and background more heavily. Keep pushing; many in similar situations have strengthened their profiles and succeeded. We're rooting for you to turn this into your story of resilience.
